summary: I moved to Vancouver in 1935 with my family, settling first in the Dunbar area. Vancouver was the site of intense labor conflicts, including the 1935 waterfront strike where I witnessed machine guns on the docks. Later in life, I returned to Vancouver for the 2014 Pacific Coast Pensioners Association convention and shared my experiences as a longshoreman and Navy veteran.

I was born in Victoria, British Columbia, in 1926, and spent much of my early life in this province. British Columbia's waterfront was a hotbed of labor activity, especially during the 1935 strike, which deeply affected my family. I also trained and served in the Canadian Navy, with time spent in Esquimalt, British Columbia, during the Second World War.

In 2014, I participated in the Pacific Coast Pensioners Association convention in Vancouver, where I gave an oral history interview about my life and work. The association represents retired longshore workers like myself, and it was important to share stories about strikes, pensions, and labor struggles. Being part of the PCPA helped me connect with others who lived through similar experiences on the waterfront.

I was a member of the International Longshore and Warehouse Union Local 500, and I walked the picket lines during the 1958 ILWU strike. The union was central to my working life on the waterfront, and I fought to have blacklisted workers from the 1935 strike reinstated in 1966. The ILWU shaped much of my career and my identity as a longshoreman in British Columbia.

I was born in Victoria, British Columbia, in 1926, where my family lived before moving to Vancouver in 1935. My father came to Victoria for Naval Reserve training, and it was a significant place in my early family history. Victoria was the starting point of my life in Canada and the backdrop for my father's naval involvement.

My father was born in Ukraine and came to Canada when he was just two years old, in 1899. Although Ukrainian heritage was part of my background, it was not openly discussed at home since my mother insisted we were Scottish. Still, growing up, I faced some discrimination possibly linked to my Ukrainian roots, especially during my youth in Vancouver.

Canada has been my home since my father immigrated here as a child, and I was born and raised here. I served in the Canadian Navy during the Second World War, traveling from the west coast to the east coast and beyond. Canada shaped my life from my early days in Victoria and Vancouver to my work on the waterfront and my military service.

My mother was born in Arbroath, Scotland, and she came to Canada when she was 18, around 1921. She married my father in 1924, and although she insisted we were Scottish, her background was a big part of our family story. Arbroath represents my mother's roots and the Scottish side of my heritage.

My father grew up on a wheat farm in Saskatchewan before going overseas in the First World War. After the war, he worked as a postmaster in Insinger, Saskatchewan, before joining the Naval Reserve and moving west. Saskatchewan was where my father’s early life and work began before he settled in British Columbia.

My father served in the First World War with the Canadian army, but because he lost finger joints as a child, he was assigned to the headquarters branch rather than combat. His service shaped his later life, including his post-war work and decision to join the Naval Reserve. The war was a significant event in my family's history and influenced their movements across Canada.

My father worked in construction in Oregon for a time, where my brother was born. However, immigration authorities told my mother she was there illegally, so we returned to Canada. Oregon was a brief but important chapter in our family's story before settling back in British Columbia.

The 1935 waterfront strike in British Columbia was a defining event in my childhood. My father was a scab during that strike, which caused tension and even violence on the docks, including machine guns being present. The strike left a lasting impact on my family and the labor community, with blacklisting affecting many workers for years.

In 1966, I was involved in efforts to get blacklisted workers from the 1935 strike reinstated, including those who had scabbed. This Victoria Day strike was part of ongoing labor struggles on the waterfront, and I fought to have these men’s time recognized for pensions. It was a moment of reconciliation and progress within the union community.

entity: 1958 International Longshore and Warehouse Union strike
summary: I participated in the 1958 ILWU strike, having worked five years on the waterfront by then. This strike was a major victory for the union, leading to the consolidation of the Canadian area and pension benefits for workers. It marked a turning point in labor relations on the Pacific Coast.

When we moved to Vancouver in 1935, we lived in the Dunbar area, which was considered a bit more upscale than typical working-class neighborhoods. Despite that, I faced fights and discrimination there, possibly because of my Ukrainian background or my father’s role as a scab. Dunbar was a challenging place for me as a young boy during those turbulent times.

I joined the Canadian Navy in 1942 when I was just 15 years and 8 months old, having lied about my age to get in. I trained in Esquimalt, British Columbia, and served on a minesweeper escorting convoys between Newfoundland, Halifax, and the eastern U.S. coast during the Second World War. My Navy service was a formative experience that took me across Canada and into American ports like Baltimore and New York City.

Early in my Navy career, I was stationed at Prince Rupert, British Columbia, where I spent about six months before applying for a gunner’s course. Prince Rupert was my first posting, and it was there I began to understand naval life and my role in the war effort. It was a stepping stone before moving to Esquimalt for further training.

I took my gunner’s course in Esquimalt, British Columbia, after serving at Prince Rupert. Esquimalt was the main naval base on the west coast and a key training center during the Second World War. Completing my course there prepared me for service on minesweepers escorting convoys on the east coast.

During my naval service in the Second World War, I was part of escort convoys that traveled to Newfoundland and Labrador as part of the triangle run. These voyages were critical for protecting shipping from enemy submarines along the Atlantic coast. Newfoundland and Labrador were important stops on our route between Canada and the eastern United States.

Halifax was another key port on the convoy route I served on during the Second World War. Our minesweeper escorted ships between Newfoundland, Halifax, and the eastern U.S., providing protection from U-boat attacks. Halifax was a busy naval hub and a critical part of the war effort on the Atlantic coast.

In September 1943, our minesweeper went into Curtis Bay, a suburb of Baltimore, for a refit. Baltimore was one of the American ports I visited during my naval service, along with Washington, D.C., and New York City. These stops gave me a chance to experience life in the U.S. during the war and connect with American sailors.

During my naval service, we had two days off in Washington, D.C., where I got to visit and even knock on the Senate building’s door with some American sailors. It was a memorable experience to be in the U.S. capital during the war and to share camaraderie with sailors from another country. Washington, D.C. was a highlight of my wartime travels.

We had three days in New York City during my naval service, which was a rare and exciting break from convoy duty. I enjoyed exploring the city and socializing with American sailors. New York City was one of the key ports on our escort route along the eastern seaboard during the war.

One night in Washington, D.C., I joined some American sailors to knock on the door of the United States Senate building, fulfilling one of their ambitions. It was a lighthearted moment amid the seriousness of war, and I often mention it when talking to Americans. That experience gave me a unique connection to the U.S. government seat during my naval service.

While escorting a tanker near the mouth of the Hudson River, we responded to a patrol boat that ran out of gas during a storm. We attempted a rescue and towed the boat to safety, demonstrating the dangers and teamwork involved in naval escort duty. The Hudson River area was part of the challenging conditions we faced on the east coast convoy routes.

Although I did not mention specific experiences at Sambro Island Lighthouse, it is located near Halifax, one of the key ports on the convoy route I served during the war. The lighthouse was an important navigational aid for ships entering Halifax harbor. It represents part of the maritime environment I was involved with during the Second World War.

While I served in the Canadian Navy, we often worked in coordination with the Royal Navy during the Second World War, especially on convoy escort duties in the Atlantic. The Royal Navy’s presence was significant in protecting shipping lanes from enemy submarines. Our minesweeper operations were part of this larger Allied naval effort.

North Vancouver was not specifically mentioned in my story, but it is part of the greater Vancouver area where I lived and worked. The waterfront labor struggles and union activities I was involved in affected the entire region, including North Vancouver. It is part of the community connected to my longshoreman experience.

I served in the Canadian Navy during the Second World War, enlisting in 1942 before I was even 16. I trained in Esquimalt and served on a minesweeper escorting convoys between Newfoundland, Halifax, and the eastern U.S. coast. The war shaped much of my early adult life and gave me experiences traveling to places like Baltimore, Washington, D.C., and New York City.
